topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The music swelled, a joyful, relentless wave washing over the crowded ballroom. I clutched my lukewarm champagne flute like a lifeline, trying to make myself smaller, invisible. Then, across the glittering expanse of dancing guests, I saw him. Standing near the cake table, laughing with someone whose hand rested casually on his arm. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake that did nothing to steady my racing pulse. Every muscle in my body seemed to lock up. My gaze snagged on him, and the world around me suddenly tilted, the bright lights blurring into an overwhelming, suffocating haze. I took an involuntary step backward, bumping into a passing waiter, and mumbled a hasty, inaudible apology, desperate to melt into the periphery of the room before he could even register my presence.

[story 2]

It was supposed to be a celebration, a happy occasion, but the atmosphere had curdled in my stomach the moment I spotted her. She was radiant, undeniably so, bathed in the soft glow of string lights. My palms immediately grew slick, clammy against the silk of my dress. I watched from behind a towering floral arrangement, my throat suddenly tight and dry. When our eyes met across the room, the sound of the orchestra seemed to drop out entirely. A cold, heavy pressure settled on my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I focused intensely on the pattern of the wallpaper, counting the intricate designs, anything to keep my attention from the sudden, alarming urge to flee the entire venue.

[story 3]

The noise level was deafening, a chaotic symphony of toasts and laughter. I was trying to navigate the throng, a clumsy island in a sea of well-dressed people, when I caught sight of him near the entrance. Time seemed to stretch, each second dragging like thick syrup. A strange, icy sensation began to creep up my spine, making the fine hairs on my arms stand rigid. My feet felt rooted to the polished floor. I deliberately turned my head away, focusing on the distant exit sign, my jaw clenched so tightly I could taste metal. Every instinct screamed at me to disappear, to vanish into the shadows, leaving this overwhelming sensory overload behind.
